Narrative: Approaching 100 kts (approximately 95 kts); we received a Master Warning and Engine 2 Fire indication. As the reject was initiated; the fire indication disappeared. After completing the Reject maneuver; we referenced the QRC and confirmed with Tower and FAs that they saw no indications of a fire. We then reviewed the Rejected Takeoff checklist in the Flight Manual; exited the runway; and had ARFF (Airport Rescue and Firefighting) look over the aircraft. After ARFF indicted that everything looked normal; we shutdown Engine 2 to taxi back using normal single-engine procedures. After parking and confirming that the aircraft was chocked; the brakes were left off in accordance with flight manual guidance. Throughout the situation; passengers were kept informed and there was no indication of any injuries. After passengers deplaned; the Captain communicated with Dispatch/Maintenance Control/Chief Pilot and the crew debriefed the event. The crew subsequently operated the scheduled flight with a different aircraft.
Result: Air Traffic Control Provided Assistance; Flight Crew FLC complied w / Automation / Advisory; Flight Crew Rejected Takeoff; Flight Crew Returned To Gate; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Maintenance Action
Synopsis: A320 Captain reported rejecting a takeoff at 95 knots due to an engine fire warning and indication on the right engine.  Crew returned to gate after CFR evaluated the aircraft.
timeline_text: During takeoff roll approaching 100 kts the crew received a Master Warning and Engine 2 fire indication; crew initiated and completed a rejected takeoff as the fire indication disappeared; crew referenced the QRC and confirmed with Tower and flight attendants that no fire was observed; exited the runway and ARFF inspected the aircraft and reported nothing abnormal; crew shut down Engine 2 and taxied back using single-engine procedures; parked and chocked the aircraft and left brakes off per the flight manual; passengers deplaned with no injuries; captain notified Dispatch/Maintenance Control/Chief Pilot and the crew debriefed; the scheduled flight was subsequently operated with a different aircraft.
normalized_summary: During the takeoff roll the crew rejected takeoff after an Engine 2 fire warning; the indication cleared, ARFF found no fire, Engine 2 was shut down, passengers deplaned without injury, and the flight was later operated on a different aircraft.

Narrative: Spot X ZZZ. After pushback.  Both engines running. Prior to after start checklist.  Fire aft cargo EICAS message with bell.Ran fire aft cargo checklist to include discharging bottles 1A and 1B.  Requested Priority Handling.  Fire light went out.  Flight attendants had no indications of smoke or fire.  Fire and rescue crews arrived and determined no apparent fire indication cargo.  Aircraft was towed back to gate XX
Result: Air Traffic Control Provided Assistance; Flight Crew FLC complied w / Automation / Advisory; Flight Crew Returned To Gate; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: B777 crew reported receiving a cargo fire EICAS message and associated alarm after engine start.  Crew discharged both fire bottles and returned to gate.
timeline_text: Pushback completed with both engines running and prior to after-start checklist; EICAS displayed 'Fire aft cargo' with bell; flight crew ran fire aft cargo checklist and discharged bottles 1A and 1B; crew requested priority handling; fire light went out and flight attendants reported no smoke or fire; fire and rescue arrived and found no apparent cargo fire; aircraft was towed back to gate XX; ATC provided assistance and flight returned to gate.
normalized_summary: An aft cargo fire EICAS message occurred after pushback; the crew discharged cargo fire bottles 1A and 1B and requested priority handling, the fire light extinguished and rescue crews found no fire, and the aircraft returned to the gate with the flight canceled or delayed.

Narrative: Prior to departure in ZZZ1 we had a fuel config light that would not stay illuminated. I wrote it up. It had been written up before and Maintenance changed the entire fuel panel to fix the fuel config light. This took a slight delay at the gate for Maintenance to fix this item. Fast forward to airborne cruise about 80 miles outside of ZZZ; I received a scratch pad FMC message that said fuel disagree. The difference was around 2000 lbs between the left and right value on progress page 2. A couple minutes prior I had turned the center tank pumps off; and at that moment I had verified that left and right tank were actually perfectly equal in quantity I transferred the flight controls to the First Officer (FO) and referenced the checklist which led me to the fuel leak checklist. I read the fuel leak checklist and was trying to determine if we indeed had a fuel leak or not. The right tank was dropping quantity very fast over 3000 pounds approaching 4000 in less than 10 minutes prior to this both left and right tanks were perfectly equal. I sent the First Officer back to look outside and he confirmed we had fuel spray off the right engine. I paused the checklist at the engine; shut down step and tried to contact Dispatch and Maintenance. Dispatch gave me a VHF frequency. I was unsuccessful in reaching Dispatch. I requested a second frequency and I still was unable to receive a transmission from Dispatch. I reverted back to text messages via ACARS; and I let Dispatch know we had a confirmed fuel leak; and we were going to shut down the right engine and we need an airport and I was requesting ZZZ that it was right in front of us and it was VFR. ZZZ2 was quite far away; and ZZZ2 was cat three at that time. I requested the weather from Air Traffic Control and I requested a lower altitude in preparation for engine shut down. I finished the checklist; calculated the landing distance; and I briefed the Flight Attendant. I made a PA and I requested priority handling with Air Traffic Control. Once the checklist was complete and the engine was secure. the fuel leak stopped. I reference the overweight; landing checklist in the diversion guide in the quick reference guide of the flight operations manual. I transferred the controls back to myself for the landing. We landed 2500 pounds overweight and the brake temperature were at four with two tires at five. I made a normal soft touchdown and had crash fire rescue inspect the aircraft on the runway. I had the First Officer make a remain seated PA; After receiving the OK from the equipment I made an announcement; expressing caring and safety; and that we will take care of each and every one of them and get them to their destination. I taxi the aircraft following an operations vehicle to the remote pack. Upon reaching the Pad; I spoke with Maintenance Control; Dispatch; and the Chief Pilot. I made additional announcements letting everyone know what was going on. It took a while for the stairs to arrive. The buses were only capable of carrying 17 persons and there was not any company personnel to assist us. I sent the FO on the first bus to have a representative in the terminal. I stayed with the aircraft and helped 17 or 18 passengers walk down the stairs and carried approximately 70 passengers luggage down the stairs because we did not have any anyone there to help us passengers were bus to the main terminal in small buses; this took an extensive time after all passengers were off. I stayed with the aircraft until the cargo was unloaded. I then shut down the aircraft and close the main cabin door ensuring the aircraft was chocked on all gears. I completed the maintenance write ups for overweight; landing; fuel leak and hot brakes.
Result: Flight Crew Inflight Shutdown; Flight Crew FLC complied w / Automation / Advisory; Flight Crew Diverted; Flight Crew Landed in Emergency Condition; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Maintenance Action
Synopsis: Air carrier Captain reported a fuel leak and subsequent engine shut down during cruise. Flight diverted and landed safely.
timeline_text: Pre-departure fuel config light would not stay illuminated; maintenance replaced the fuel panel at the gate causing a slight delay; at cruise ~80 nm out received FMC fuel disagree (~2000 lb) and observed right tank quantity dropping rapidly (>3000–4000 lb in <10 minutes); FO confirmed fuel spray from the right engine and crew initiated the fuel leak checklist; crew attempted VHF contact with Dispatch without success, switched to ACARS to notify Dispatch and requested diversion to ZZZ, lower altitude, and ATC priority; completed checklist, performed inflight shutdown of the right engine and the fuel leak stopped; calculated overweight landing distance, briefed cabin, transferred control for landing and landed 2500 lb overweight with elevated brake temperatures and a soft touchdown; emergency services inspected the aircraft, taxied to a remote pad, passengers deplaned via stairs and buses, crew remained until cargo was offloaded and then shut down and chocked the aircraft; maintenance write-ups completed for fuel leak, overweight landing, and hot brakes.
normalized_summary: During cruise the crew detected a rapid fuel loss and confirmed fuel spraying from the right engine; they followed the fuel leak checklist, shut down the right engine inflight, diverted to ZZZ and landed 2500 pounds overweight. Emergency services inspected the aircraft, passengers were deplaned, and maintenance write-ups were completed.

Narrative: Right after takeoff between 800' and 1000' the throttles began to retard as airspeed bug had dropped down to the top of the amber band (Minimum Maneuvering Speed). The auto throttle was disconnected and the MCP (Mode Control Panel) speed window was opened to set a higher speed. The FO and Relief Pilot confirmed that we did NOT  have an unreliable airspeed indication. After completing the After Takeoff Checklist; we used FLCH; V/S and ALT HOLD to complete the climb to cruise altitude.During the climb; we tried multiple times to select VNAV; but it would always result in the airspeed bug dropping to the top of the amber band and had to resort to other MCP modes.  Attempts to change cruise speed or CI (Cost Index) values had no effect on the the airspeed bug.We contacted Dispatch who brought in Maintenance Control and Fleet Support to attempt to troubleshoot the problem with no success.With poor weather and low ceilings forecast at destination and alternates; we elected to return to ZZZ. Fuel dumping was NOT required to land below Max Landing Weight. Landing at ZZZ was uneventful; but VNAV seemed to struggle with calculations.
Result: Air Traffic Control Provided Assistance; Flight Crew Returned To Departure Airport; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Maintenance Action
Synopsis: Air carrier flight crew reported experiencing the auto throttles retard while on departure; resulting in the flight crew disengaging the auto throttles to regain control of the aircraft's speed. The flight crew returned to the departure airport where they landed safely.
timeline_text: Shortly after takeoff (800–1000') the throttles began to retard as the airspeed bug dropped to the top of the amber (Minimum Maneuvering Speed) band; crew disconnected autothrottle and opened the MCP speed window to set a higher speed; FO and Relief Pilot confirmed no unreliable airspeed indication; after completing the After Takeoff checklist, crew used FLCH, V/S and ALT HOLD to continue climb; repeated attempts to engage VNAV caused the airspeed bug to drop and VNAV would not maintain speed, so crew used other MCP modes; changes to cruise speed or Cost Index had no effect on the airspeed bug; dispatch, maintenance control, and fleet support were contacted but could not resolve the issue; due to poor weather at destination and alternates, crew elected to return to ZZZ; fuel dumping was not required and landing at ZZZ was uneventful.
normalized_summary: During initial climb the airspeed bug repeatedly dropped to the top of the amber (minimum maneuvering) band and VNAV/autothrottle would not maintain or respond to speed/Cost Index changes, so the crew disconnected autothrottle and used other MCP modes. The flight returned to the departure airport (ZZZ) uneventfully; maintenance and fleet support could not resolve the issue and the flight was cancelled/delayed.

Narrative: Fuel Leak was observed 1 hour into ZZZ1-ZZZ2 Flight Segment.   The flight was planned for a maximum weight landing in ZZZ2.  The cruise speed was systematically increased to give a projected landing fuel load of ~500 lbs below max landing weight.  If there was a fuel leak in the early portion of the flight; it may have been hidden due to our dynamic planning and the fuel coming from the center tanks.   Shortly after completion of center tank fuel burn off; a scratchpad message indicating a difference between measured and calculated fuel state indicated that there was an anomalous fuel state.  We noticed a near 1000 lb wing tank fuel differential immediately; and saw that the measured and calculated fuel used had a differential that was larger than this.  Control of the aircraft was exchanged to myself; the FO; and the captain began to run the Fuel Leak non annunciated procedure.  Controls were exchanged again to allow me to go after and look for visual indications.  Looking  the wing through one of the bulkhead windows revealed a mist trail that I would describe as 2 feet in diameter and opaque with a quality to condensation trails that for when landing in high humidity areas.   Back in the flight deck I reported I was able to visually confirm that I could see fluid leaving the aircraft.  The fuel imbalance and anomalies had grown; and procedure directed shutdown was accomplished.  The aircraft was prepared for an overweight single engine landing in ZZZ on the longest runway available.   An unremarkable landing was completed and after inspection by emergency services; we continued under our own power to the remote parking location.  After approx 1 hour of ground delay; passengers began disembarking via air stair.  I went into the terminal to provide an early morning representative for the company; and the captain remained onboard the aircraft.  There were significant delays in unloading passengers and baggage due to limited ground resources.  The aircraft was secured by the captain roughly 4-5 hours after landing.
Result: Flight Crew Diverted; Flight Crew Inflight Shutdown; Flight Crew Landed in Emergency Condition; Flight Crew Overcame Equipment Problem; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: B757 First Officer reported a fuel leak that resulted in a divert and overweight landing.
timeline_text: Flight planned with increased cruise speed to reduce landing fuel load; ~1 hour into ZZZ1-ZZZ2 segment crew observed fuel leak; after center tank burnoff scratchpad message showed a measured vs calculated fuel discrepancy; crew noted ~1000 lb wing tank fuel differential and larger measured/calculated fuel-used difference; control exchanged while captain ran Fuel Leak non-annunciated procedure; narrator visually inspected and observed an opaque mist trail and reported fluid leaving the aircraft; fuel imbalance increased and procedure-directed shutdown was accomplished; aircraft prepared for and conducted an overweight single-engine landing at ZZZ on the longest runway; emergency services inspected the aircraft and it taxied under its own power to remote parking; after ~1 hour ground delay passengers disembarked and the aircraft was secured ~4–5 hours after landing.
normalized_summary: Approximately one hour into the flight the crew detected and visually confirmed an in-flight fuel leak, performed procedure-directed shutdown, and diverted to ZZZ for an overweight single-engine landing. The aircraft landed uneventfully, was inspected by emergency services, taxied to remote parking under its own power, and was secured about 4–5 hours later.

Narrative: When reviewing the logbook it was discovered that the previous crew had done an air return based on unreliable speed.  A special flight permit was then issued based on ADR1 being inoperative.  The logbook stated that an inspection had been done by Maintenance of the TAT1 (Total Air Temperature) prob; no defects were noted; and the aircraft was signed off to ferry the aircraft and aircraft was safe for flight.  Under the other restrictions section of the special flight permit stated; follow Manual pro/abnormal and emergency procedures for ADR1 fault.  Based on the logbook entry of the aircraft safe for flight; I took this statement as follow the Manual procedure if ADR1 fault to occur.  Based on previous crews write-up; Captain briefed me to pay close attention to airspeed disagreement on takeoff and we both came to an agreement that we will abort if a speed discrepancy is noted.  On takeoff role; me the FO; was the pilot PM; said 80 kts and  Captain noted the airspeed was only at 50 kts so he performed the abort.  We exited the runway and called dispatch to coordinate a gate return.  While waiting for ground personnel to get in place;  Captain spoke with Maintenance control about the abort and he stated we needed to preform the Manual abnormal procedure and that's what the special flight permit is for.  Soon after; dispatch sent us an ACARS telling the ramp crew was ready to bring us into the gate.  After arriving at the gate and talk with the captain we agreed to call Maintenance Control and verify exactly what we were to do.  He confirmed to follow abnormal ADR1 procedures which after doing so; showed no airspeed; altitude; or vs on the captain side.  The Captain and I both agreed that this is unsafe and therefore refused the aircraft in the interest of safety. The ferry permit guidance in the Operations Manual could be more detailed on procedures to follow.  Also; this specific permit should have been more informative on what to expect and do.  I feel I could have also called maintenance before departure for clarification on what is expected which would have prevented us departing in the first place.  I feel this ferry permit was unsafe and should not have been issued.Ferry Permit issuance and listed procedure created an aircraft to be unsafe for departure.
Result: Flight Crew Returned To Gate; Flight Crew Rejected Takeoff; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Maintenance Action; General Work Refused; General Release Refused / Aircraft Not Accepted
Synopsis: With an inoperative air speed indicator; the FO reported the ferry permit and aircraft were refused as unsafe by the Captain.
timeline_text: Previous crew performed an air return for unreliable speed and a special flight permit was issued for ADR1 inoperative; logbook entry stated Maintenance inspected the TAT1 probe, found no defects, and signed the aircraft safe for ferry with instructions to follow Manual abnormal/emergency procedures for ADR1 fault; Captain briefed FO to monitor for airspeed disagreement and abort if noted; on takeoff roll FO called 80 kts while Captain observed 50 kts, and Captain aborted takeoff; crew exited runway, coordinated a gate return with dispatch, and contacted Maintenance Control who instructed performing the Manual abnormal ADR1 procedure per the permit; at the gate, executing the abnormal ADR1 procedure resulted in no airspeed, altitude, or vertical speed on the captain's side; crew judged the aircraft unsafe, refused the aircraft, and the flight was canceled with maintenance action and release refused
normalized_summary: A ferry permit allowed departure with ADR1 inoperative; an airspeed disagreement on takeoff prompted an aborted takeoff and, after performing the ADR1 abnormal procedure at the gate which removed key flight indications on the captain's side, the crew refused the aircraft and canceled the flight.

Narrative: After engine start; during the flight control check we noticed binding when Turing the yolk to the left and then back to neutral.  We notified mx (Maintenance); returned to the gate and made a write up.  Mx performed their checks and everything passed.  However; the binding was still present when my FO and I checked the flight controls again.  In the interest of safety; we decided to refuse the aircraft.
Result: Flight Crew Returned To Gate; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Release Refused / Aircraft Not Accepted
Synopsis: Air carrier Captain reported that while performing the flight control check after engine start; the Captain discovered binding in movement. Flight returned to gate and the aircraft was refused.
timeline_text: After engine start; during the flight control check crew noticed binding when turning the yoke left and back to neutral; crew notified maintenance and returned to the gate and made a writeup; maintenance performed checks and reported all checks passed; pilots rechecked controls and binding remained; crew refused the aircraft
normalized_summary: During the preflight flight control check the yoke exhibited binding when turned left and returned to neutral; despite maintenance checks that passed, the binding persisted and the crew refused the aircraft, resulting in return to gate and flight cancellation/delay.

Narrative: We were flying from ZZZ1 to ZZZ2 and when the event started we were cruising at FL350 off the coast. Everything in the flight before that had proceeded normally; but the event started with a call from FA3; I believe; informing of us of a strong and terrible odor between rows 12 and 23. It had caused on the FAs (flight attendants) to vomit and feel light headed to the point of passing out. We later found out that 2 of the other FAs noticed the smell and were getting headaches. At that point the Captain and I knew we were going to need to divert; thinking this a potential fume event; especially with the FA getting so ill. I took control of the aircraft and radios while the Captain called Dispatch through his iPad to discuss with them. Dispatch agreed with out plan of diverting to ZZZ; so I informed ATC of our request as well as the reason for the diversion. The Captain then elected to request priority handling; we decided with the information we had; it would be prudent to get priority handling to get the aircraft on the ground and receive any needed medical assistance. I was flying at this point and got the aircraft setup for the approach in ZZZ while the Captain spoke with the FAs and informed the pax of what was going on. The Captain took control of the aircraft again while I kept the radios and helped with communication back and forth with dispatch through ACARS. Once we were caught up getting everything set up and briefed for the diversion; we started looking at the QRH for a fume event. At this point we hadn't smelled anything and we both felt fine; we followed the QRH and since the odor was determined to be in the cabin elected to turn off pack 2; I think we were around 11000 to 13000 feet on the arrival at this point. Pack 2 stayed off the rest of the flight; partly was for the time required in the procedure and then we were getting into the approach phase and our focus was more on getting the aircraft on the ground safely. We had received our gate; so we requested XXC to minimize taxi time. It was until short final the we both started getting a faint odor; it had apparently been spreading throughout the cabin at that point as well; as reported by the FA in the rear of the aircraft. We had not received any complaints from passengers about feeling ill; and it was intermittent on whether they smelled it or not. Once we landed and taxied off the runway; I turned pack 1 off as well out of precaution since we had started to smell a dirty socks type odor. We taxied to the gate without issue; where we were met with the requested medics.Cause: One thing the Captain and I discussed after the event; was it probably would have been a good idea to proactively don the masks even though we didn't start to smell anything until the very end of the flight.
Result: Air Traffic Control Provided Assistance; Flight Crew Diverted; Flight Crew Landed in Emergency Condition; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: Air carrier First Officer reported an inflight diversion due to fumes in the cabin. Flight landed at diversion airport.
timeline_text: Cruising at FL350 off the coast when a flight attendant reported a strong odor between rows 12 and 23 that caused FAs to vomit, feel lightheaded, and one to pass out; crew decided to divert and Dispatch agreed to divert to ZZZ; PF took control and handled radios while the Captain coordinated with Dispatch, the FAs, and requested priority handling; crew briefed, consulted the QRH for a fume event, and turned off pack 2 around 11,000–13,000 ft which remained off for the remainder of the flight; a faint odor spread through the cabin on short final; after landing the crew turned off pack 1 as a precaution, taxied to the gate, and met requested medics
normalized_summary: A suspected cabin fume/odor event caused multiple flight attendants to become ill; the flight crew diverted to ZZZ, followed QRH procedures (pack 2 off), landed with priority handling, and met medical personnel at the gate.

Narrative: At approximately 600 feet agl; on the upwind; Flight Instructor (IP) noticed door open annunciation and realized the rear door was cracked open and then IP went to immediately close rear door but it unlatched and within seconds; flew/ripped clean off. IP took controls and Student Pilot (SP) held down bags and items in rear seat while we flew back to runway XX. Door is suspected to be in the ocean. We landed safely and taxied back. No damage to the tail or any other part of the plane.
Result: Aircraft Aircraft Damaged; Flight Crew Landed in Emergency Condition; Flight Crew Returned To Departure Airport; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: DA40 student pilot reported that the rear door separated from the aircraft during climb. Flight landed safely.
timeline_text: At approximately 600 ft AGL on the upwind; instructor observed door-open annunciation and found the rear door cracked open; instructor attempted to close the rear door but it unlatched and flew off within seconds; instructor took control while the student held down bags and items in the rear seat; crew returned to runway XX and landed safely and taxied back; door is suspected to be in the ocean and no damage was observed to the tail or other parts
normalized_summary: The rear passenger door unlatched and separated in flight at about 600 ft AGL; the instructor took control, the crew returned to the departure airport and landed safely with no damage observed to the tail or other parts.

Narrative: Discontinued takeoff due to engine failure. Had trouble trying to make comms with Airport Rescue and Firefighting (ARFF) when they arrived. Cause: Working engine.
Result: Flight Crew Rejected Takeoff; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: A321 NEO flight crew reported a rejected takeoff due to an engine failure.
timeline_text: Attempted takeoff; discontinued takeoff due to reported engine failure; ARFF arrived; crew had trouble making communications with ARFF; crew rejected takeoff and flight was cancelled or delayed.
normalized_summary: Crew discontinued takeoff due to reported engine failure and had trouble communicating with ARFF; the takeoff was rejected and the flight was cancelled or delayed. The report lists the cause as "working engine".

Narrative: Starting a 90 degree right turn on ramp.  Traffic (tug that pulls the baggage carts) was northbound; traffic appeared after we started our turn. FO states traffic is not slowing down I started applying the brakes to slow.  The FO states he's not stopping I then stopped the aircraft to prevent a collision.  I called the back to see how they; FA's; were doing.  The number one said he hit his head and was bleeding.  Taxied back to the gate. Cause: If rampers stopped for approaching aircraft. And if the AC (Aircraft) could have been pushed back further into the ramp. We would not have been so close to the road the ramp traffic drives on.
Result: Flight Crew Returned To Gate; Flight Crew Took Evasive Action; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Physical Injury / Incapacitation
Synopsis: Air carrier Captain reported having to stop the aircraft due to a ground vehicle not yielding. Captain reported a Flight Attendant injury and subsequent return to the gate.
timeline_text: Started a 90-degree right turn on ramp; a northbound tug appeared after the turn began and did not slow; crew applied brakes and then stopped the aircraft to prevent a collision; cockpit crew checked the cabin and a flight attendant reported hitting his head and bleeding; taxied back to the gate.
normalized_summary: Crew stopped the aircraft during a ramp turn to avoid a collision with a northbound tug; the aircraft returned to the gate and one flight attendant sustained a head injury with bleeding, resulting in flight delay/cancellation.

Narrative: On takeoff from ZZZ in accordance with company takeoff SOP; upon reaching approx. 300 - 400 both ECU (Engine Control Unit) A and B annunciations appeared. Crew immediately requested priority handling due to the windy conditions; low altitude; and possible imminent engine loss. Crew immediately notified tower they would be conducting a low level right turn for a low right pattern to land on [runway] XX. Upon starting the turn; ECU A extinguished but ECU B was latched. Crew completed the necessary and appropriate checklists and ran QRH. Crew then accomplished successful landing with taxi to FBO with no further occurrences to report.
Result: Flight Crew FLC complied w / Automation / Advisory; Flight Crew Landed in Emergency Condition; Flight Crew Returned To Departure Airport; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: DA40 pilot reported a dual loss of the Engine Control Unit on initial climb. Flight returned to the field and accomplished a successful landing.
timeline_text: Takeoff from ZZZ in accordance with company SOP; at approx. 300 - 400 both ECU A and B annunciations appeared; crew requested priority handling due to windy conditions, low altitude, and possible imminent engine loss; crew notified tower they would conduct a low-level right turn for a low-right pattern to land on runway XX; during the turn ECU A extinguished but ECU B remained latched; crew completed required checklists and ran the QRH; crew landed successfully and taxied to the FBO with no further occurrences.
normalized_summary: On initial climb after takeoff from ZZZ both ECU A and B annunciations illuminated, with ECU B latched; the crew requested priority handling, ran checklists/QRH, executed a low-right return to the departure airport, and landed safely, taxiing to the FBO.

Narrative: I entered Aircraft X with my student after reviewing his preflight preparation for a cross country flight from ZZZ to ZZZ1. Heading out to the aircraft; which was already preflighted by my student; nothing appeared out of the ordinary. What wasn't noticed was the tow bar left in the front of the aircraft. We entered the aircraft and ran the before start and engine start checklist. When my student engaged the aircraft starter; the propeller struck the tow and launched it approximately 10 to 15 feet to the left. My student disengaged the starter and shut down the aircraft. A small dent is on the propeller; and the engine is pending review by the aircraft's owners.
Result: Aircraft Aircraft Damaged; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Maintenance Action
Synopsis: PA-28 Flight Instructor and student reported the propeller striking the tow bar upon engine start. Flight was cancelled.
timeline_text: Reviewed student's preflight; entered aircraft which had been preflighted by student; tow bar left in front of aircraft was not noticed; ran before-start and engine-start checklists; student engaged starter and propeller struck the tow bar, launching it about 10–15 feet to the left; student disengaged starter and shut down engine; small dent observed on propeller and engine is pending review; flight cancelled/delayed and maintenance action required.
normalized_summary: During engine start the propeller struck a tow bar left in front of the aircraft, launching it ~10–15 feet and causing a small dent to the propeller; the flight was cancelled/delayed and maintenance/engine review is pending.

Narrative: Flight from ZZZ1 to ZZZ airport under day VFR.  Flight was uneventful and weather was beautiful.  Landed on runway XX and taxied to the transient ramp. I stopped my airplane perpendicular to the ramp directly in front of my desired tiedown spot so that I was positioned correctly to push back into the spot.  As I was accomplishing the engine shutdown checklist; my right seat passenger alerted me to an aircraft which was rapidly approaching from the right. By the time I looked up; the wingtip of the taxing aircraft was about two seconds away from striking my propeller. Before I could shut my engine down (which was at idle; with the rotating beacon still on); Aircraft Y's left wingtip struck my propeller sending bits and pieces into my cowling; windshield and elevator as well as the ramp area in front of my plane. Aircraft Y stopped about 100 feet later and shut down. The pilot came over to my aircraft to apologize and admitted the incident was entirely his fault. He said his airplane had just completed its annual inspection and he was just taxiing it back to his hangar. He was watching his wingtip clearance to his right and said he never looked to his left where I was located. He also stated he hadn't felt well that day and seemed a bit dazed by what had just happened. Contributing factors were his physical/mental health being less than ideal; his failure to adequately clear his taxi route and taxiing too fast. My propeller substantially damaged approximately 12 inches of his left wingtip. My prop and windshield were also damaged. My engine will require an inspection to determine if it was damaged.
Result: Aircraft Aircraft Damaged; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Maintenance Action
Synopsis: Pilot reported a taxiing aircraft collided with their parked aircraft on the ramp.
timeline_text: Flight from ZZZ1 to ZZZ landed on runway XX and taxied to the transient ramp; pilot stopped aircraft perpendicular to the ramp in front of desired tiedown and began engine shutdown checklist; right-seat passenger alerted pilot to an aircraft rapidly approaching from the right; before engine could be shut down, Aircraft Y's left wingtip struck the propeller, sending debris into the cowling, windshield, elevator and ramp; Aircraft Y stopped about 100 feet later and shut down; Aircraft Y pilot admitted fault, citing not clearing his taxi route, taxiing too fast, and feeling unwell; propeller and windshield were damaged, approximately 12 inches of Aircraft Y's left wingtip was substantially damaged, and the engine will require inspection; result recorded as aircraft damaged, flight canceled/delayed, and maintenance action required
normalized_summary: While stopped on the transient ramp after landing, another aircraft taxied into the right side and its left wingtip struck the stopped aircraft's propeller. The collision damaged the propeller, windshield and elevator, substantially damaged about 12 inches of the other aircraft's left wingtip, and requires an engine inspection; the incident resulted in flight cancellation/delay and maintenance action.

Narrative: The flight was planned from ZZZ2 to ZZZ3 to ZZZ4 and then back to ZZZ2. Overall; the flight was planned for just under 3 hours. Weather and airfield information supported our flight. The preflight; ground operations; and initial departure were uneventful. After takeoff; ATC permitted navigation and altitude at our discretion. We climbed to 7500 FT enroute to ZZZ3. Approximately 40 minutes into the flight; I detected abnormal engine sounds and reduced engine performance. I then immediately took action to maintain positive aircraft control while the pilot not flying continued to analyze the instruments. After recognizing the issue was engine related; I told the pilot not flying to find the nearest airport for a precautionary landing. The closest airport was ZZZ1. I began a turn directly to ZZZ1 while capturing the appropriate glide airspeed per the flight manual. While I was doing this the pilot not flying began checklist procedures for engine roughness/misfire. After the pilot not flying completed the checklist; engine performance was remedied. From there we could once again maintain airspeed and altitude. With the added engine performance; I began a climb as we approached ZZZ1 in case the engine completely failed. After the pilot not flying completed the emergency and normal checklists; the pilot not flying continued to gather information about ZZZ1 to prepare for the landing. As we continued to ZZZ1; we realized that we might be able to safely arrive at ZZZ. ZZZ was more suitable because unlike ZZZ1; the runway was longer; paved and illuminated. As such; we continued to climb enroute to ZZZ1 to maximize options so that if the engine failed we would still be able to land at ZZZ1 but also if we could gain enough altitude we could safely arrive at ZZZ with safer landing condition. Once the pilot not flying said we gained enough altitude to safely glide to ZZZ; we began heading to ZZZ. During this time the pilot not flying continued to coordinate with ATC and requested priority handling. Later ATC cleared us to switch to CTAF frequency. The pilot not flying made the CTAF calls to announce our intentions. The pilot not flying also activated the runway lighting system. I continued to maintain positive control of the aircraft and performed a safe landing on Runway XX. After landing we taxied to park and shutdown the engine. After shutdown; the pilot not flying immediately informed ATC of our arrival by phone. I do not know what caused the issue but using the checklist greatly helped the situation.
Result: Air Traffic Control Provided Assistance; Flight Crew Landed in Emergency Condition; Flight Crew Diverted; Flight Crew Overcame Equipment Problem; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: A C172 pilot reported a rough running engine and diverted to a nearby suitable airport.
timeline_text: Flight planned ZZZ2→ZZZ3→ZZZ4→ZZZ2 and departed uneventfully; climbed to 7,500 ft enroute to ZZZ3; ~40 minutes into flight detected abnormal engine sounds and reduced engine performance; PF maintained control while PNF analyzed instruments and identified engine issue and selected nearest airport ZZZ1; turned toward ZZZ1 and captured glide airspeed while PNF ran engine roughness/misfire checklist; checklist remedied engine performance and airspeed/altitude were maintained; crew climbed to retain options and elected to proceed to ZZZ (longer, paved, illuminated runway) while coordinating with ATC; switched to CTAF, activated runway lighting, and landed safely on Runway XX; taxied to parking, shutdown engine, and PNF notified ATC by phone
normalized_summary: Approximately 40 minutes into cruise the crew experienced engine roughness/reduced engine performance; after executing checklists and coordinating with ATC they diverted to a longer, paved, illuminated airport (ZZZ) and landed safely, then shut down and notified ATC.

Narrative: During pushback; flight attendants reported an electrical burning smell in the aft cabin; but it dissipated quickly.  After second engine start the entire aircraft including the flight deck filled with strong fuel fumes.  Immediate return to gate. All crew members taken to hospital for tests of carbon monoxide poisoning.   All crew members had varying levels of carbon monoxide poisoning.  Crew deadheaded back following day.Cause: Repeat write up on this aircraft and known airbus issue.
Result: Flight Crew Returned To Gate; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Physical Injury / Incapacitation
Synopsis: A321 pilot reported fumes in cabin and cockpit during push back resulting in a return to the gate. Crew members went to the hospital where they were found to have signs of CO poisoning.
timeline_text: During pushback flight attendants reported an electrical burning smell in the aft cabin which dissipated quickly; after second engine start the entire aircraft including the flight deck filled with strong fuel fumes; the flight immediately returned to the gate; all crew members were taken to hospital for carbon monoxide tests and were found to have varying levels of carbon monoxide poisoning; crew deadheaded back the following day.
normalized_summary: After second engine start the aircraft filled with strong fuel fumes, prompting an immediate return to the gate; crew were hospitalized and found to have varying levels of carbon monoxide poisoning, and the flight was canceled/delayed.

Narrative: The flight was delayed due to maintenance swapping the number 1 and 3 ADIRUs and deferring the number 3. All indications were normal as we taxied to and lined up on runway XX. I set the thrust in the flex temp and cross checked the instruments; indications were normal.  As the aircraft built speed going down the runway; I could tell that my airspeed indications were slow to come up. At the 80 kt check; my airspeed had just started to register. At about the same time; I got red flashing FD (Flight Director) and multiple ECAM cautions. I called the abort and brought the thrust levers to max reverse.  I tried to disconnect the auto brakes and take over manually to make the abort less violent; but they wouldn't disconnect.  We came to a stop on the runway with the auto brakes still in max. I told my FO to disconnect the brakes; he cycled the anti-skid switch and the brakes disconnected.  I gave the remain in your seats PA to the flight attendants and passengers.  We exited the runway and taxied back to the gate. On our first leg; ZZZ1 to ZZZ; we got an unreliable airspeed on takeoff. We followed the procedure and determined that the airspeed on the captains PFD (Primary Flight Display) was unreliable. We switched Air Data to Capt 3 on the Switching panel and that restored my airspeed.  I ACARSed dispatch and let them know of the problem and that we would need maintenance in ZZZ. We continued to ZZZ without further incident. That was the reason for the maintenance in ZZZ; we can't defer ADIRU 1.  Whatever the mechanics did in ZZZ; made the problem worse.
Result: Flight Crew Rejected Takeoff; Flight Crew Returned To Gate; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: A320 flight crew reported unreliable airspeed indicator during takeoff roll. Flight crew rejected takeoff.
timeline_text: Maintenance swapped ADIRU 1 and 3 and deferred ADIRU 3; taxied and lined up with indications normal and set flex thrust; during takeoff roll airspeed indications were slow to come up and at 80 kt airspeed had just started as red flashing FD and multiple ECAM cautions appeared; crew rejected the takeoff and selected max reverse; autobrakes remained engaged at max and would not disconnect until the FO cycled the anti-skid switch; crew gave remain-seated PA, exited the runway and taxied back to the gate; flight was subsequently canceled/delayed.
normalized_summary: During the takeoff roll unreliable/slow airspeed indications and multiple ECAM cautions led the crew to reject the takeoff; the aircraft stopped on the runway with autobrakes stuck at max but were later disconnected and the flight returned to the gate. The flight was canceled/delayed.

Narrative: At approximately 130 kts we received a master warning engine failure message. I rejected the take off and we accomplished the rejected takeoff off checklist and all appropriate checklists. After CFR (Crash Fire Rescue) cleared the aircraft we cleared the runway to let the brakes cool. After brakes cooled we were towed to the gate
Result: Air Traffic Control Provided Assistance; Flight Crew Returned To Gate; Flight Crew Rejected Takeoff; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: B777 flight crew reported engine failure on takeoff roll. Flight crew rejected the takeoff.
timeline_text: At approximately 130 kts received a master warning engine failure message; rejected the takeoff and completed the rejected takeoff checklist and all appropriate checklists; Crash Fire Rescue (CFR) cleared the aircraft; cleared the runway to let the brakes cool; after brakes cooled the aircraft was towed to the gate
normalized_summary: During the takeoff roll at about 130 kts the crew received an engine-failure master warning and rejected the takeoff, completing required checklists. After CFR cleared the aircraft they cleared the runway, cooled the brakes, and were towed back to the gate, resulting in return to gate and flight delay/cancellation.

Narrative: After a standard pushback and a normal start on engine two the First Officer proceeded to start engine one. The First Officer stated that N2 was not observed. The First Officer then questioned if the bleeds were configured correctly. We verified the configuration to be correct. We then attempted another start on engine one; once again no N2 was observed along with a starter valve open indication. We then entered a logbook entry for engine failure to start with no N2 observed. We called Dispatch and asked for a patch with Maintenance Control. Maintenance Control notified us that a gate return was necessary. After coordinating with Operations and Ramp that we needed to return to the gate. Approximately five minutes had passed a master fire alarm for the number one engine illuminated along with the associated bells. We then actioned ENGINE OVERHEAT; immediate action items. That directed us to the ENGINE FIRE or ENGINE SEVERE DAMAGE or SEPARATION checklist; followed by the QRC/QRH. We then advised that we had an indication of a fire in the number one engine and requested ARFF (Airport Rescue and Firefighting). I then called the purser to view the number one engine and see if there was any indication of smoke or fire. The purser stated no observable; fire or smoke; and she stated that she would keep an eye on the number one engine. Ramp gave no indication of observable; fire or smoke. ARFF arrived and indicated there wasn't any observable fire or smoke. We then coordinated a gate return after Airport Rescue and Firefighters indicated all clear.
Result: Flight Crew FLC complied w / Automation / Advisory; Flight Crew Returned To Gate; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: B737 flight crew reported an overheat and subsequent fire indication on engine during push back. Crew secured engine and returned to gate.
timeline_text: Pushback and engine two start normal; First Officer attempted engine one start and observed no N2; bleed configuration verified correct; second start attempt on engine one again showed no N2 and a starter valve open indication; crew logged engine failure to start and contacted Dispatch and Maintenance Control, who required a gate return; coordinated return with Operations and Ramp; approximately five minutes later master fire alarm and associated bells for engine one illuminated; crew executed ENGINE OVERHEAT immediate actions and followed the ENGINE FIRE/SEVERE DAMAGE/SEPARATION checklist and QRC/QRH; requested ARFF and purser/ramp observed no visible fire or smoke; ARFF confirmed no observable fire or smoke and cleared; crew returned to the gate and the flight was canceled/delayed.
normalized_summary: Engine one failed to start (no N2) and a subsequent master fire alarm prompted execution of engine overheat/fire procedures; ARFF found no fire, the aircraft returned to the gate, and the flight was canceled/delayed.

Narrative: We completed preflight duties; push back; and taxi out uneventfully. When cleared for takeoff I set 40% N1 and allowed the engines to stabilize. I then stood the thrust levers up and selected the TOGA switch. When the auto throttles engaged; I felt the left thrust lever advance to a greater TLA (Throttle Lever Angle) than the right and the aircraft began to be pushed to the right as a result of the asymmetrical thrust. I called for a rejected takeoff; performed the appropriate actions; and ran the RTO (Rejected Takeoff) checklist. I estimate our top speed during the takeoff roll to be 20 knots. We contacted maintenance and ultimately elected to return to the gate where maintenance personnel pulled the aircraft from service in order to complete inspections and repairs as needed.
Result: Flight Crew Rejected Takeoff; Flight Crew Returned To Gate; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Maintenance Action
Synopsis: Air carrier Captain reported a rejected takeoff due to asymmetrical thrust. Flight returned to gate.
timeline_text: Completed preflight duties; push back and taxi out uneventful; cleared for takeoff, set 40% N1 and allowed engines to stabilize; stood thrust levers up and selected TOGA; autothrottles engaged and left thrust lever advanced to a greater TLA than the right, causing the aircraft to be pushed to the right; crew called rejected takeoff, performed RTO actions and ran the RTO checklist (estimated top speed ~20 knots); contacted maintenance and returned to the gate where maintenance pulled the aircraft from service for inspections/repairs.
normalized_summary: Autothrottle asymmetry during TOGA caused the left thrust lever to advance more than the right, pushing the aircraft to the right. The crew executed a rejected takeoff at about 20 knots, returned to the gate, and maintenance removed the aircraft from service for inspection/repair.

Narrative: During pushing inflight crew called and reported there was a burning smell in the aft of the aircraft and that they were in the process of locating the source. After the second engine started; inflight informed us the smell had become stronger. In the flight deck the smell of fuel became very clear. After a discussion and evaluating the situation we determined that returning to the gate was the safest option for the passengers and crew. After deplaning the entire crew was transported to the hospital and was found to have higher than normal levels of CO2 in our blood.Cause: Repeat write up for this aircraft. The only way to prevent this from happening again is to locate source of fumes.
Result: Flight Crew Returned To Gate; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Evacuated
Synopsis: Air carrier pilot reported while being pushed back from the gate being advised from the cabin crew of a burning smell in the aft of the aircraft and the flight crew experienced a fuel smell in the flight deck resulting in the flight crew returning to the gate and deplaning the passengers and crew.
timeline_text: During pushback crew reported a burning smell in the aft of the aircraft while locating the source; after the second engine started crew reported the smell had become stronger; in the flight deck a fuel smell became very clear; crew discussed and decided returning to the gate was the safest option; aircraft returned to the gate and passengers deplaned/evacuated; entire crew transported to the hospital and found to have higher-than-normal CO2 levels; flight was cancelled/delayed.
normalized_summary: Crew reported a burning/fuel smell during pushback that intensified after the second engine start, prompting a return to the gate; passengers were deplaned/evacuated and the crew was taken to hospital with elevated CO2 levels, and the flight was cancelled or delayed.

Narrative: During normal arrival phase in weather ATC vectored us behind a 777 to intercept the final approach course at 2;500 feet outside of ZZZZZ; cleared us for the approach; and instructed us to maintain 180 IAS to ZZZZZ1 (FAF). As PM I noticed the decreasing range to the heavy aircraft in front and instructed the FO (First Officer) (PF) to start slowing 2 miles early; however on switch to the Tower the Tower Controller told us the separation wasn't going to work and directed a go around at approximately 1;400 AGL to 2;000' and runway heading. We were IMC almost the whole time. FO did a great job and the GA and subsequent approach were normal.Cause: It was a rainy; windy; low visibility day in the area. Approach Controller put us in a high workload corner and the Tower Controller made the GA call since we were IMC. The obvious mitigation is more separation with heavy 777 traffic. It just didn't work today for an approach however we had fuel and time on our side. I could tell the strain on ATC is starting to show. They remained professional and noticeably measured in tough conditions.
Result: Air Traffic Control Issued New Clearance; Flight Crew Executed Go Around / Missed Approach; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: B737 Captain reported ATC issued go around instructions on short final due to less than required spacing behind a preceding heavy jet.
timeline_text: ATC vectored aircraft behind a 777 to intercept final approach course at 2,500 ft outside ZZZZZ; cleared for the approach and instructed to maintain 180 IAS to ZZZZZ1 (FAF); PM noted decreasing range to the heavy and instructed FO (PF) to start slowing 2 miles early; on switch to Tower, Tower advised separation wouldn't work and directed a go-around at approximately 1,400 AGL to 2,000' and runway heading; FO executed the missed approach/GA in IMC; subsequent approach was normal.
normalized_summary: Flight was vectored behind a heavy 777 in IMC, but Tower determined separation was insufficient and directed a go-around at about 1,400 AGL; the crew executed the missed approach and later completed a normal subsequent approach.

Narrative: When taking the active Runway 20L in BKK we suddenly noticed rocks covering the takeoff area and giant chunks of asphalt. It seems that previous departing aircraft blew a hole in the runway. I turned to taxi past it on the left edge of the runway and notified Tower. Aircraft Y's crew said our right engine went over an area of rocks. So we shut down the right engine and taxied back to the gate. We wrote it up to FOD check both engines. Aircraft signed off after inspection and engine run. Cause: Previous aircraft blew a hole in the runway on takeoff. It appeared that the hole was from a previous patch.
Result: Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; Flight Crew Returned To Gate; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Maintenance Action
Synopsis: Air carrier Captain reported a previous departing aircraft blew a hole in the runway at BKK and due to the rocks and giant chunks of asphalt now present on the takeoff area; the flight crew taxied back to the gate to check for potential FOD damage.
timeline_text: During takeoff on Runway 20L crew noticed rocks and large chunks of asphalt covering the takeoff area; observed a hole in the runway believed to be blown by a previous departing aircraft; turned to taxi along the left edge and notified Tower; another crew reported our right engine went over an area of rocks; crew shut down the right engine and taxied back to the gate; wrote up the event for FOD check of both engines; maintenance inspected, performed an engine run, and signed the aircraft off.
normalized_summary: While taking Runway 20L the crew encountered rocks and large asphalt chunks from a runway hole believed to have been caused by a previous departure; after the right engine ran over rocks the crew shut it down, returned to the gate, requested ATC assistance, and maintenance inspected and signed the aircraft back into service, causing delay.

Narrative: Abnormal vibrations were felt in CA and FO rudder pedals during climb out of ZZZZ.  The vibrations were constant but irregular. They were high frequency and made our feet tingle/sting when on the rudder pedals.  I called the flight attendants and asked them to check the cabin for any unusual vibrations.  At first they reported none; then they reported hearing and feeling vibrations in the forward galley and around row 9.  FO stayed as PF and took over the radios while I contacted Dispatch and Maintenance via SATCOM.  Maintenance saw nothing unusual in their systems. MX (Maintenance) initially hinted they wanted us to continue to ZZZ; but we were not certain it was prudent to continue. So next they hinted at going to ZZZ or ZZZ1.  However upon consultation with entire crew; the decision was made to divert to ZZZ2.  At this point we were almost equal time from ZZZZ and ZZZ2; and ZZZ2 is a Maintenance base.  The crew agreed this was a good choice.  We ran the diversion checklist and overweight landing checklist  No code was given; no emergency was declared.  Overweight landing at 176;200 lbs (321Neo). 100fpm at touchdown.  We requested and were cleared to land on ZZZ2 Rwy XX; since it was longer that Rwy XY. CA flew the approach and landing due to the overweight condition.  Logbook entires for vibration and overweight landing.  Vibration Report filled out in full detail.  Debriefed and discussed at length with maintenance control.  Flight continued to ZZZ three hours later.  Passengers were kept informed throughout the diversion; delay at ZZZ2; and on the continuation of the flight.  They were generally appreciative of being kept informed and understood we put safety first.
Result: Air Traffic Control Provided Assistance; Flight Crew Diverted; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; General Maintenance Action; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: Air carrier Captain reported vibrations in the rudder pedals and flight attendant reported vibrations in the cabin. Flight diverted to nearest airport as a result.
timeline_text: Abnormal high-frequency vibrations felt in CA and FO rudder pedals during climb out of ZZZZ; flight attendants checked cabin and reported vibrations in forward galley and around row 9; FO remained PF and handled radios while CA contacted Dispatch and Maintenance via SATCOM; Maintenance reported no anomalies and initially suggested continuing to ZZZ or ZZZ1; crew consulted and decided to divert to ZZZ2 (a maintenance base), roughly equal time from ZZZZ and ZZZ2; crew ran diversion and overweight landing checklists and requested/received clearance to land on ZZZ2 Rwy XX (longer than Rwy XY); CA flew the approach and performed an overweight landing at 176,200 lbs with a 100 fpm touchdown; no emergency was declared; logbook and vibration report were completed and the flight continued to ZZZ three hours later
normalized_summary: Abnormal high-frequency vibrations were felt in the pilots' rudder pedals and reported in the cabin during climb out from ZZZZ; the crew diverted to maintenance base ZZZ2 and conducted an overweight landing (176,200 lbs) without declaring an emergency, then continued to ZZZ three hours later.

Narrative: We were dealing with an APU not doing a great job of cooling the aircraft while on the ground in ZZZ. The cockpit temperature never got below 90 and the cabin remained at 84/82. Knowing we had a 20 minute flight back to ZZZ1; we elected to continue. Performance mandated we take off in the opposite direction in ZZZ using runway XX. The push was delayed 10 minutes for ground traffic out to runway XX. Startup and taxi went normally but the interior started heating up after engine start. Temps hovered at 98 in the cockpit and 88/84 in the cabin. We were hoping to hustle because once airborne the plane would start cooling off. Ground told us takeoff would be delayed at least 20 minutes due to arriving and departing traffic.After a 10 minute sit waiting for takeoff; we got an Master Caution Light and ECAM message stating a fuel overheat condition in the left wingtip fuel tank. The temperature was 57 C and in less than 20 seconds it climbed to and hit 72 C. We accomplished the ECAM actions shutting down the left generator and left engine. We were now obligated to return to the gate.We asked for a taxi back but ground stated we could not taxi back for at least 20 minutes. In the meantime the right wingtip fuel was overheating hitting about 58 C when we made the decision to advise ATC to gain traffic priority. We got an immediate back taxi down the runway and returned to our pad location. We accomplished the remaining ECAM actions once parked; shutting down the right generator and right engine. The temp reached 68C in the right tank at shutdown. Cabin air temps remained at the previous highs of 98/88/84 during the whole incident.I exited the cockpit and asked the passengers to quickly but orderly depart the aircraft down the front and rear airstairs. We were concerned about how high the temperatures actually went in the fuel tanks. Ground fire fighting crews rolled and monitored the fuel tank temperatures. The passengers were calm and the exit was swift and orderly. I asked my inflight crew to go with the passengers and had my first officer manage the arriving staff while I cleaned up the remaining checklist items and shut down the aircraft.
Result: Air Traffic Control Provided Assistance; Flight Crew FLC complied w / Automation / Advisory; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; Flight Crew Returned To Gate; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: A320 Captain reported returning to the gate after noting high fuel temperatures during taxi.
timeline_text: APU cooling inadequate on ground and crew elected to continue for short flight; push delayed and during startup/taxi interior temperatures rose; while holding for takeoff a Master Caution and ECAM reported left wingtip fuel overheat with rapid temperature rise; crew followed ECAM, shut down left generator and left engine and decided to return to the gate; ground initially would not allow taxi back and right wingtip began overheating, so crew requested ATC priority and received immediate back-taxi to the pad; once parked crew completed ECAM actions shutting down right generator and right engine; passengers were evacuated via front and rear airstairs while fire crews monitored fuel tank temperatures
normalized_summary: During a pre-departure delay the crew received fuel overheat warnings in the wingtip tanks; they shut down the affected generators and engines, returned to the gate, evacuated passengers, and ground fire crews monitored tank temperatures.

Narrative: Safety Report - Jet Bridge Contact Incident at Gate XX A/C XDate of Incident: Day 0Location: Gate XXSubject: Jet Bridge Contact with Aircraft Door 1L and Subsequent Management ConductSummary of Event:On Day 0; maintenance personnel were dispatched to Gate XX in response to an incident in which the jet bridge contacted the aircraft at the 1L boarding door. The contact resulted in the door being lifted approximately three to five (3-5) inches above its normal seated position.Upon inspection; maintenance personnel expressed concern regarding potential damage to the door hinges and the possibility of hidden or non-visible structural damage caused by the vertical displacement. Given the nature of the contact and the potential safety implications; maintenance deemed it necessary to perform a thorough evaluation prior to determining the aircraft's airworthiness.Concerns Raised:During the course of the event; members of management were observed pressuring maintenance personnel to expedite the evaluation and sign off the discrepancy. It was also stated that individuals outside the maintenance department had characterized the mechanics' assessment as exaggerated. Furthermore; management insinuated that maintenance personnel were engaging in a job action" or "work slowdown;" implying that the delay in clearing the aircraft was deliberate rather than safety-driven.Safety Implications:Such behavior from management undermines the integrity of the maintenance decision-making process and creates an environment where personnel may feel pressured to prioritize operational schedules over safety and regulatory compliance. This incident highlights an ongoing concern regarding conflicting priorities between maintaining on-time performance and ensuring that all aircraft discrepancies are properly inspected and resolved in accordance with maintenance standards and FAA regulations.Conclusion:Maintenance personnel acted in accordance with established procedures and their regulatory responsibility to ensure aircraft safety prior to return to service. The interference and insinuations from management during this incident are inconsistent with the company's stated commitment to a "safety first" culture and should be reviewed to prevent recurrence.Suggested Resolution:  Reinforce Maintenance Authority and Safety Protocols; Reiterate to all operational and management personnel that only qualified maintenance staff have the authority to determine airworthiness and sign off mechanical discrepancies. Provide refresher training or communication emphasizing that safety-related inspections cannot be influenced or expedited by non-maintenance personnel; Reaffirm the company's commitment to a "safety first" culture by addressing instances of pressure or interference promptly and transparently."
Result: General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Maintenance Action
Synopsis: Air carrier technician reported they were dispatched to a B737-800 at the gate to evaluate possible damage to door 1 left due to the jet bridge contacting the door and lifting it.
timeline_text: Jet bridge contacted aircraft at 1L boarding door, lifting the door approximately 3-5 inches; maintenance personnel dispatched to Gate XX and inspected the door; maintenance raised concerns about possible hinge and hidden structural damage and required a thorough evaluation before determining airworthiness; management pressured maintenance to expedite the evaluation and sign off, and insinuated a deliberate work slowdown; maintenance followed procedures and withheld clearance pending evaluation; flight cancelled/delayed and general maintenance action recorded.
normalized_summary: A jet bridge contacted the aircraft at door 1L, lifting the door 3–5 inches; maintenance withheld return-to-service pending further evaluation for potential hinge/structural damage while management pressured staff. The event resulted in a flight cancellation/delay and general maintenance action.

Narrative: Crew sent an ACARS to Maintenance Control advising of low oil quantity; down to 9%. I began looking at the QRH for something related to low oil quantity. The crew advised me that they had low oil and might be diverting. I acknowledged their message and asked if the engine was shut down or running. After a few minutes; there was still no reply; I asked if they would like a patch with Maintenance Control and recommended ZZZ as a precautionary diversion; unaware if the engine was shut down or not. A few minutes after; I advised the crew that ZZZ1 was approximately 150NM off of their nose and sent them the weather. At the same time; they advise the oil quantity was down to 6% and was also thinking of ZZZ1. I agreed with the crew about the diversion to ZZZ1 and the process was initiated. I released the flight to ZZZ1 without the MEL; unaware if the engine was running or not; knowing I could add it after if necessary. I asked the crew again; asking which engine; if it was shut down; and if an emergency was declared; they only advised it was still running. I sent the crew normal landing data based on the running engine instead of non-normal landing data and did not have communication with them again until after they landed.ZZZ1 ATCT was advised prior to landing that the flight was inbound with engine trouble; but they were already aware. I later followed up with the ATCT to confirm priority handling was requested and ARFF (Airport Rescue and Firefighting) vehicles were standing by for their landing.
Result: Air Traffic Control Issued New Clearance; Air Traffic Control Provided Assistance; Flight Crew Diverted; Flight Crew Landed As Precaution; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; Flight Crew Overcame Equipment Problem; Flight Crew Landed in Emergency Condition; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: An air carrier Dispatcher reported one of their flights experienced low oil quantity in one engine and diverted to a nearby airport.
timeline_text: Crew sent ACARS reporting low oil quantity (9%); ATC reviewed QRH and queried whether the engine was shut down or running; after no immediate reply, ATC recommended ZZZ1 as a precautionary diversion and provided weather; crew later reported oil down to 6% and agreed to divert to ZZZ1; ATC released the flight to ZZZ1 without an MEL, asked again which engine and if it was shut down, and was told the engine was still running; ATC provided normal landing data based on a running engine and coordinated with ZZZ1 ATCT for priority handling and ARFF standby; flight landed at ZZZ1 as a precaution.
normalized_summary: The flight reported low engine oil (decreasing from 9% to 6%) and diverted to ZZZ1; ATC coordinated the diversion, provided landing data based on a running engine, arranged ARFF standby, and the aircraft landed there as a precaution.

Narrative: We were at cruise altitude of 35;000 feet approximately 64 miles east of ZZZ when we heard a loud bang form of noise. It sounded like when an EFB mount falls and hits the ground. I looked up and saw my windshield (FO) side had shattered. I mentioned to the captain that we may have to divert. He looked over and saw the windshield and agreed. Seconds later we received an EICAS windshield heater inoperative message. We identified/canceled and ran the QRH for both the eicas and windshield broken checklists. In the midst of that the captain elected to request priority handling as a precaution in case of the spider web style of shatter would get worse and ultimately crack the inner layer. We knew ZZZ was a base; close in proximity; and a maintenance base so that is where we elected to go. I believe we handled the situation as best as trained ultimately following our guidelines of do not panic; fly the airplane; and communicate. In the midst of the event all parties were notified. Flight Attendants; Dispatch; ATC; and the passengers. The outcome was best as possible with no additional damage; injuries; and safety first actions being taken place. After being on the ground we were told there was a plane for us on the next gate over. After finishing what needed to be done we set up the new plane and ultimately finished the route to ZZZ1 and brought the passengers where they intended to go.
Result: Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; Flight Crew Landed in Emergency Condition; Flight Crew Diverted; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Maintenance Action
Synopsis: Embraer 170 First Officer reported the First Officer's window outer pane shattering during cruise flight. The flight diverted to an alternate airport and landed safely with no injuries.
timeline_text: Cruising at 35,000 ft about 64 miles east of ZZZ; loud bang heard and the first officer side windshield shattered; EICAS displayed windshield heater inoperative; crew identified/canceled messages and ran QRH checklists for EICAS and windshield broken; captain requested priority handling and elected to divert to nearby maintenance base ZZZ; ATC, dispatch, flight attendants, and passengers were notified; aircraft landed in an emergency condition with no additional damage or injuries; passengers were moved to a replacement aircraft and the flight continued to ZZZ1.
normalized_summary: The first officer side windshield shattered at cruise, the crew ran applicable QRH procedures, requested priority handling, and diverted to the nearby maintenance base where they landed in an emergency condition with no injuries or further damage. Passengers were transferred to a replacement aircraft and the flight continued to its destination.

Narrative: We had a normal taxi to the runway. Once we were at maximum speed; the pilots hit the brakes. We were stopping hard and items were sliding down the aisle. Once we came to a complete stop; our Captain came over the PA and said to remain seated. We shouted the command and assessed conditions outside the plane and checked on customers. I did not yell 'Brace; brace; brace' and didn't hear anyone in my immediate area either. On our ride to the hotel we discussed this and weren't sure that we did the right thing since we couldn't see why were we were braking. There could have been something in from of us. I'm used to training where we hear crashing sounds and people screaming once we start yelling commands.
Result: Flight Crew Rejected Takeoff; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: Air Carrier flight attendants reported a high speed rejected takeoff due to engine malfunction.
timeline_text: Normal taxi to the runway; at maximum speed the pilots applied brakes; aircraft stopped hard and items slid down the aisle; Captain announced to remain seated; crew shouted a command, assessed conditions outside and checked on customers; flight crew rejected takeoff and the flight was cancelled/delayed, passengers transported to hotel
normalized_summary: Pilots applied brakes at maximum taxi speed, resulting in a hard stop with items sliding and the crew assessing conditions; the takeoff was rejected and the flight was cancelled/delayed, with passengers taken to a hotel.

Narrative: About an hour into the flight (ZZZ-ZZZ1); we noticed engine no. 1 had a low oil qty of 12% and decreasing 1%; every few minutes. Reviewed manuals and found no guidance; so we messaged dispatch and Maintenance Control. Maintenance Control only said; 'follow the manual'; which I found surprising. We immediately descended to a lower altitude; in case the engine failed; we'd already be at an appropriate altitude and we thought the lower thrust setting while descending would help preserve the remaining oil. Decided on diverting to ZZZ2. Kept descending to keep thrust low and eventually kept no. 1 engine at idle for same reason for remainder of the flight. A few times; eng (Engine) oil qty reached 0 (zero); but pressure and temperature remained normal; entire time. Briefed many times; if engine failed; but it didn't and we landed safely.
Result: Air Traffic Control Provided Assistance; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; Flight Crew Landed As Precaution; Flight Crew Landed in Emergency Condition; Flight Crew Diverted; Flight Crew FLC complied w / Automation / Advisory; General Maintenance Action; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: B737 flight crew reported loss of engine oil during cruise. Flight diverted and landed safely.
timeline_text: About an hour into the flight noticed engine no. 1 had low oil quantity (12%) and was decreasing about 1% every few minutes; reviewed manuals and, finding no guidance, messaged dispatch and Maintenance Control who advised to 'follow the manual'; immediately descended to a lower altitude and reduced thrust to preserve remaining oil and be at an appropriate altitude if the engine failed; decided to divert to ZZZ2 and kept engine no. 1 at idle for the remainder of the flight; oil quantity reached zero a few times while oil pressure and temperature remained normal; briefed crew repeatedly for possible engine failure and landed safely at the diversion airport
normalized_summary: Engine 1 exhibited a steadily decreasing oil quantity about an hour into the flight; the crew descended, reduced thrust, consulted maintenance, diverted to ZZZ2, kept the engine at idle, and landed safely with no engine failure despite intermittent zero oil quantity indications.

Narrative: During climbout from ZZZ; GPS-L invalid message started to intermittently come on and frequently. Crew decided with Maintenance and Dispatch confirmation that to land back in ZZZ would be the best decision due to nature of approaches in the area and due to now lack of redundancy in the GPS system. With crew concurrence; the plane was landed overweight since ZZZ was partly IMC; over speed of about 3 kts on flaps 30 setting was also noted due to gusty conditions. Landing was not hard; though both overweight landing and over speed were written up so that Maintenance may inspect. Expected hot brakes and advised Operations of situation.
Result: Air Traffic Control Provided Assistance; Flight Crew Landed in Emergency Condition; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; Flight Crew Returned To Departure Airport; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: B737 flight crew reported loss of a GPS unit on climbout. Flight returned to departure airport and landed.
timeline_text: Climbout from ZZZ; GPS-L invalid message began to come on intermittently and more frequently; Crew consulted Maintenance and Dispatch and decided to return to ZZZ due to loss of GPS redundancy and local approach considerations; Aircraft landed back at ZZZ overweight in partly IMC with about 3 kts overspeed on flaps 30 in gusty conditions; Landing was not hard and overweight/overspeed were written up for maintenance; ATC provided assistance and Operations were advised; Flight was canceled/delayed.
normalized_summary: During climbout from ZZZ a GPS-L invalid message led the crew, with Maintenance and Dispatch concurrence, to return and perform an overweight landing with a slight flaps-30 overspeed; the landing was not hard, maintenance write-ups were completed, ATC assisted, and the flight was canceled/delayed.

Narrative: I completed the preflight walk around prior to departure and did not observe the nose gear pin flag hanging below the nose gear doors. On takeoff; when the Captain called for gear up; I moved the gear lever to the up position and observed that the nose gear was showing in transit. A Caution ECAM then alerted us to the L/G Nosegear Not Unlocked. We attempted to cycle the gear and got the same response. We then left the gear down and requested priority handling. We received vectors around while the Captain worked through his steps; while I flew. We landed underweight and safely back in ZZZ. Once at the gate; the mechanics found that the nose gear pin was left in from an earlier tire and brake change and the flag was only about 8 inches long and was not visible below the gear doors. Cause: Longer and Brighter Gear Pin Flag and maintenance verification of all pin removals.
Result: Air Traffic Control Provided Assistance; Flight Crew Returned To Departure Airport; Flight Crew Overcame Equipment Problem; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; Flight Crew Landed in Emergency Condition; General Maintenance Action; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: A320 flight crew reported nose gear did not retract on take off due to the nose gear pin being left in.
timeline_text: Completed preflight walk-around and did not observe nose gear pin flag; took off and when commanded to retract gear moved lever up and saw nose gear in transit; Caution ECAM 'L/G Nosegear Not Unlocked' alerted crew; attempted to cycle gear with same result; left gear down and requested priority handling; received vectors while captain ran procedures and first officer flew; landed underweight and safely back at ZZZ; at the gate mechanics found the nose gear pin left in and the flag was only about 8 inches long and not visible below the gear doors.
normalized_summary: A maintenance-installed nose gear pin remained in place, preventing nose gear unlocking; the crew kept the gear down, requested priority handling, and landed safely back at the departure airport, where mechanics found the short flag.
