Narrative: I was captain and pilot flying Aircraft X ZZZ-ZZZ1  We pushed back from gate XX and taxied to runway XXL and departed to our destination.  We knew the weather was forecast was calling for snow and fog and visibility was at ILS minimums.  While on approach at 7;000 ft MSL; we received a R WING ANGI ICE Caution message.  The FO and I ran the QRH procedure for that particular message.  The message still persisted after completing the procedure and the QRH directed us to leave icing conditions.  To accomplish this; we climbed the aircraft to 16000 feet and were able to get on top of the cloud layer.  We messaged dispatch of our situation and returning to ZZZ was our best option as the weather was clear for the rest of the flight.  The rest of the flight had no further events and landed safely back at ZZZ.Cause: Failure in the wing anti ice system which caused the EICAS message.Suggestions: The same aircraft was written up previously that day in ZZZ1 for the same EICAS message but was signed off by local maintenance.  More  thorough systems testing before signing the aircraft off.
Result: Flight Crew Landed in Emergency Condition; Flight Crew FLC complied w / Automation / Advisory; Flight Crew Returned To Departure Airport; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: CRJ-900 Captain reported failure of a wing anti icing system during descent. Flight returned to departure airport.
timeline_text: Pushed back from gate, taxied and departed to destination; while on approach at 7,000 ft MSL received R WING ANTI ICE Caution; flight crew ran QRH procedure which did not clear the message; QRH directed leaving icing conditions so crew climbed to 16,000 ft to get on top of the cloud layer; crew notified dispatch and decided to return to departure airport ZZZ; landed back at ZZZ with no further events.
normalized_summary: A right wing anti-ice caution message occurred during approach, caused by a wing anti-ice system failure; the crew executed QRH procedures, climbed to 16,000 ft to exit icing, and returned to land safely at the departure airport in an emergency condition. The flight was cancelled/delayed.

Narrative: Pushback commenced normally with a standard sequence of events. We were given clearance to push from gate XXX to spot A. Captain gave command 'brakes released cleared to push spot A.' Pushback crew read back appropriate instructions. A minute into the pushback the left wing marsheller gave the command to stop pushback and the tug driver did so in a smooth manner. The flight deck was then told that the bottom of the left wing tip had come into contact with a fuel truck antenna. At time of occurrence and position of aircraft there was no indication from the flight deck that it had made contact with anything. Maintenance was called. Maintenance determined to take the aircraft out of service. The pushback crew was replaced and the new crew towed the aircraft back to gate XXX. Passengers deplaned and moved to different gate/ aircraft.
Result: Aircraft Aircraft Damaged; Flight Crew Returned To Gate; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: B737 flight crew and ramp agent reported the aircraft wingtip stuck an antennae of a fuel truck during pushback; resulting in damage to the aircraft wingtip.
timeline_text: Pushback commenced from gate XXX to spot A after captain command 'brakes released cleared to push spot A'; pushback crew read back the instruction; one minute into pushback the left wing marshaller ordered stop and the tug driver stopped smoothly; flight deck was informed the bottom of the left wing tip had contacted a fuel truck antenna, though the flight deck had no indication of contact; maintenance was called and determined to take the aircraft out of service; the pushback crew was replaced and the new crew towed the aircraft back to gate XXX; passengers deplaned and were moved to a different gate/aircraft.
normalized_summary: During pushback the bottom of the left wing tip contacted a fuel truck antenna, resulting in aircraft damage; maintenance took the aircraft out of service, it was returned to the gate, and passengers deplaned and were moved to another gate/aircraft.

Narrative: As Captain I refused to operate Aircraft X from ZZZZ to ZZZ on Day 0.  The aircraft arrived with inoperative HMG (Hydraulic Motor Generator) and 10 other deferrals.  The follow on crew apparently refused the aircraft also. Boeing designed the aircraft with an operable HMG for a reason; as the backup in the unlikely event of dual AC bus failure.  Our flight was dispatched 120minute ETOPS; being over two hours from any airport would be in mind one of those times that backup should be required.   There is a warning in our flight manual that states:  If the HMG fails or is inoperative; flight beyond 30 minutes will result in a complete loss of electrical power and the inability to lower the gear and flaps.  When flying domestically with many landing options within this time frame; this would be perhaps acceptable.  However on this flight that was not the case.
Result: General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Release Refused / Aircraft Not Accepted
Synopsis: B767 Captain reported refusing an aircraft that had an inoperative back up generator.
timeline_text: Captain refused to operate Aircraft X from ZZZZ to ZZZ on Day 0; aircraft arrived with inoperative HMG and 10 other deferrals; follow-on crew also refused the aircraft; flight was dispatched as 120-minute ETOPS (over two hours from any airport); flight manual warns HMG failure or inoperative beyond 30 minutes results in complete loss of electrical power and inability to lower gear and flaps; flight cancelled/delayed and aircraft not accepted.
normalized_summary: Captain refused to accept Aircraft X because the Hydraulic Motor Generator was inoperative (plus 10 deferrals) while the flight was dispatched as 120-minute ETOPS and the manual warns loss of electrical power beyond 30 minutes; the flight was cancelled/delayed and the aircraft was not accepted.

Narrative: We pushed back from gate A; the tug was disconnected and engine #2 was started. After ground crew was disconnected we performed our after start checks. During the flight control check the operation of the elevators and ailerons did not feel correct. There was a significant grab/sticking in the movement of the controls. I had the first officer operate the controls to verify what I was feeling. I had recently heard of this happening; specifically on the Max aircraft. As a crew; we made the decision; it was not safe to fly. We called maintenance frequency and said we are returning to the gate. We were immediately met with pushback and told; 'this is not convenient'. That statement was not safe; caring or dependable. After parked back at the gate; the aircraft technician met us. We told her what we experienced and she immediately began saying what we felt was normal. Neither the first officer or I had experienced this flight control issue before. I explained that I had recently heard several pilots reporting this same issue and it is a known issue and there is a known fix. She had no interest in what we had to say and she repeated numerous times it was normal. We did not agree. I made a call to Maintenance Control and Maintenance Control said this is a known issue and this aircraft needs to be fixed. I went back to the flight deck to check in with the technicians. They proceeded to push for us to fly the aircraft and said all was normal. I stated I called Maintenance Control and they agreed that this is not normal and it needed to be fixed. She pushed again for us to take the aircraft. I said I was not comfortable taking it. She got mad; defensive and very rude and stormed off of the aircraft. She was extremely un professional in front of a plane full of passengers and very rude to me. This was a safety concern and she pushed us to continue. We swapped to a different aircraft; also a max 9. We did the control check on it and they operated correctly.
Result: Flight Crew Returned To Gate; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed; General Release Refused / Aircraft Not Accepted; General Maintenance Action
Synopsis: B737 MAX 9 Captain reported unusual stiff flight control movement when performing the flight control check on the ground after engine start. The Captain refused the aircraft for flight.
timeline_text: Pushed back from gate A; tug disconnected and engine #2 started; after-start checks performed; during flight control check elevators and ailerons exhibited significant grab/sticking; first officer operated controls to verify; crew decided aircraft was not safe to fly and contacted maintenance, then returned to the gate; local technician insisted controls were normal and urged flight but Maintenance Control confirmed this is a known issue requiring a fix and the aircraft was not accepted; crew swapped to a different max 9 and its control check was normal
normalized_summary: During preflight control check the elevators and ailerons exhibited significant grab/sticking; the crew returned to the gate, refused the aircraft, and Maintenance Control required a maintenance fix, resulting in the flight being delayed/cancelled. The crew swapped to another max 9 whose controls checked normal.

Narrative: Besides a slight delay due to the aircraft not being towed into the gate everything went normal from the boarding process to the pushback and taxi out. As we got taxi instructions and started taxing to runway XXL ground asked if we can accept XXL at Taxiway 1; we ran the numbers and replied that we can do it; also at the same time we realized we might be departing quicker than expected and started the 2nd engine. After running through all our flows and checklists right after I made the PA for the flight attendants to be seated we heard some kind of alarm (beeping noise) unsure of where it was coming from for a second; I looked up and saw the red smoke indication on the Aft Avionics smoke panel; as soon as I saw it I verbalized it and the Captain immediately reached over and pressed the power button on the aft avionics switch shutting the system down which got rid of the alarm and smoke indication. The Captain called the cabin crew and they said they also had an indication and they would ask the other cabin crew if they smelled anything and would call us back. I notified ATC that we have an issue and cannot take the runway at the moment; they gave us a place to park at which point the Captain contacted maintenance over the radio. They advised us to contact Maintenance Control. While the Captain was getting in contact with Maintenance Control I made a PA to the passengers and one of the cabin crew members called us and said she did smell a bit of an electric smoke odor as if from an electrical spark. I called operations and let them know that we need to coordinate a gate for us to come back. Maintenance Control advised us to go back to the gate just as we planned. Once the Captain was off the phone with Maintenance Control I notified operations and ATC that we are going back to the gate. We taxied back to gate XXX and deplaned the aircraft. Neither I nor the Captain noticed any odor in the flight deck. Our only indication was the alarm and smoke light on aft avionics panel.
Result: Air Traffic Control Issued New Clearance; Flight Crew Overcame Equipment Problem; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; Flight Crew Returned To Gate; General Flight Cancelled / Delayed
Synopsis: A321 First Officer reported a smoke alarm for the aft avionics panel while taxiing and a return to the gate.
timeline_text: Slight delay at gate due to aircraft not being towed into the gate; boarding, pushback and taxi-out proceeded normally; ATC asked if crew could accept runway XXL at Taxiway 1 and crew accepted; crew started the second engine anticipating earlier departure; after flows, checklists and a PA for cabin to be seated an alarm sounded and the aft avionics smoke panel showed a red smoke indication; Captain powered down the aft avionics which silenced the alarm and cleared the indication; cabin crew reported a slight electrical smoke odor; crew notified ATC, contacted maintenance and Maintenance Control who advised returning to the gate; crew taxied back to gate XXX and deplaned; flight deck crew did not notice any odor.
normalized_summary: During taxi an aft avionics smoke alarm and red smoke indication occurred; the crew powered down the aft avionics, notified ATC and maintenance, returned to the gate and deplaned. The alarm/indication ceased after shutdown and no odor was observed in the flight deck.
